items.title: Social media users experience more political hostility in less economically equal and less democratic societies
items.author: Michael Bang Petersen
items.content: Drawing on survey data from 30 countries, Bor et al. find that online political hostility is higher in more unequal and less democratic societies.

items.title: A systematic review of the global and regional estimates of the prevalence of sexual violence against children
items.author: Inga Vermeulen
items.content: A global analysis of 1,412 studies shows that one in five women and one in six men experienced sexual violence as children, highlighting the urgent need for evidence-based prevention and intervention programmes worldwide.

items.title: Premature termination of unemployment benefits increased COVID-19 transmission and deaths in the USA
items.author: John S. Earle
items.content: Park et al. show that discontinuing pandemic unemployment insurance in 2021 tripled rates of COVID-19 cases, hospitalizations and deaths in the USA. Expanded unemployment insurance served as a non-pharmaceutical intervention by incentivizing physical distancing during a severe pandemic.

items.title: Unlearning research microbehaviours from authoritarian backgrounds
items.author: Myint Thu
items.content: Researchers from authoritarian academic backgrounds often exhibit self-censoring microbehaviours, which cause difficulties when transitioning to nonauthoritarian environments, explains Myint Thu.

items.title: The overlooked threat of democratic neutrality in the USA
items.author: Brittany C. Solomon
items.content: Hall et al. document ‘democratic neutrality’ (neither agreement nor disagreement with undemocratic practices) as prevalent and as consequential as outright support for undemocratic practices in shaping preferences for anti-democratic candidates.

items.title: The effect of praise from peers on empathy and political inclusion towards racial or ethnic outgroups
items.author: Lotem Bassan-Nygate
items.content: Longitudinal US experiments show that peer praise for empathy can promote politically inclusive behaviour towards racial/ethnic outgroups. Varying effects among subgroups suggest the value of targeted rather than uniform approaches.

items.title: Sustainable neuroscience through open science
items.author: Helena Hartmann
items.content: Neuroscience is crucial for understanding human behaviour. Yet, its resource-intensive methods contribute to the climate crisis. We call on neuroscientists to align their research with ecological sustainability goals across the research cycle and propose three key steps: replace unfocused data collection, reduce excessive emissions and refine imprecise methods.

items.title: Measuring thresholds for individual change can improve social change interventions
items.content: Behavioural and complexity sciences have largely proceeded separately in studying social change. By reinterpreting social contagion dynamics through discrete-choice modelling, we demonstrated how thresholds for individual change can be estimated from choice data, and theoretically examined how they can improve interventions for social change.
